title: 10 best herbal teas for queasy stomach
intro: When it comes to soothing a queasy stomach, certain herbal teas can offer fast and effective relief. Peppermint tea is a popular choice due to its calming and antispasmodic properties, which can help reduce stomach discomfort and cramping. Ginger tea, with its natural anti-inflammatory and digestive qualities, is also a great option for easing nausea and settling the stomach. Chamomile tea, known for its soothing and relaxing effects, can also help alleviate digestive issues and promote overall gut health. Lastly, licorice root tea can help protect the stomach lining, reduce inflammation, and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach. Whether you're looking for immediate relief or long-term gut health support, these herbal teas offer a natural and delicious remedy for a queasy stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea's soothing warmth and mild flavor make it an ideal remedy for a queasy stomach. Ginger contains active ingredients, such as gingerols and shogaols, which possess well-documented anti-inflammatory and digestive properties. These compounds work together to help neutralize stomach acids, alleviating discomfort and reducing the likelihood of nausea. Furthermore, ginger tea stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, enhancing nutrient absorption and promoting overall gastrointestinal health. Lastly, ginger's natural calming effects on the intestinal tract help relax muscles, providing lasting relief from queasiness and occasional indigestion.
tea_parts: Roots: Contain gingerols and shaogals, compounds with proven antiemetic properties that help alleviate feelings of nausea.
Rhizomes: These horizontal underground stems are rich in gingerols and shaogals, which make them effective in treating symptoms of queasy stomach.
Leaves: High in flavonoids and polyphenols, they possess anti-inflammatory properties, providing relief from digestive discomfort.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants, they help soothe the stomach and reduce inflammation.
Seeds: Though less potent than the other parts, they still contain beneficial compounds that can aid in alleviating queasy stomach.
tea_recipe: Peel and thinly slice 1 inch of fresh ginger root.
In a small saucepan, combine ginger slices with 2 cups of water and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, then stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey and a squeeze of lemon juice (if desired).
Drink warm, up to three times a day for queasy stomach relief.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that help relax the intestinal tract, providing relief from nausea and an upset stomach.
Shogaols: Shogaols are known for their antiemetic properties that help alleviate feelings of vomiting and queasiness in the stomach.
Zingerone: Zingerone aids in soothing the stomach lining, reducing inflammation, and providing relief from stomach discomfort and cramps.
Paradols: Paradols have antispasmodic effects that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, providing comfort and reducing symptoms of a queasy stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea relieves a queasy stomach due to its therapeutic properties. The menthol present in peppermint tea soothes the stomach by relaxing gastrointestinal muscles. This action allows the stomach to move food through the digestive system more efficiently, reducing queasiness. Peppermint tea also has antispasmodic qualities, which alleviate discomfort by minimizing intestinal spasms. Furthermore, peppermint tea's calming effect on the stomach lining reduces inflammation and provides quick relief from queasiness.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in menthol and menthone, peppermint leaves aid in digestion and provide quick relief from queasy stomach.
Flowers: High in flavonoids, peppermint flowers help relax gastrointestinal tract muscles, alleviating discomfort and unease in the stomach.
Seeds: Packed with essential oils, peppermint seeds support healthy digestion and soothe symptoms of queasy stomach.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, add boiling water to 1 tablespoon of dried peppermint leaves, allowing the tea to steep for 10 minutes.
While waiting, warm a mug by rinsing it with hot water.
Strain the tea into the warmed mug to remove the leaves.
Optionally, add 1 teaspoon of honey for added sweetness and throat soothing.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to gently soothe your queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Menthol: Menthol has antispasmodic properties that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing stomach discomfort and queasiness.
Mentone: Mentone stimulates the production of bile and gastric juices, aiding in digestion and alleviating queasy stomach.
Limonene: Limonene has carminative properties that help reduce bloating and gas, providing relief for queasy stomach.
Cineol: Cineol, also known as eucalyptol, has anti-inflammatory effects that can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce queasiness.
Pulegone: Pulegone acts as a mild anesthetic, numbing the stomach lining and providing temporary relief for queasy stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea brings relief to a queasy stomach due to its antispasmodic and carminative properties. This herbal tea contains volatile oils and flavonoids that target the digestive system, relaxing the smooth muscles in the stomach lining and intestines, thus alleviating discomfort. Its anti-inflammatory properties further soothe any inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract. Moreover, chamomile tea promotes the production of mucin, protecting the stomach lining from acid and bile. Lastly, chamomile's calming effects extend beyond the digestive system, reducing anxiety and stress that can exacerbate gastrointestinal issues.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers contain calming properties that help soothe a queasy stomach.
Leaves: Chamomile leaves hold necessary oils with anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic qualities, beneficial for addressing queasy stomach.
Seeds: Rich in therapeutic compounds, chamomile seeds bring about relaxation and relief to an upset stomach.
tea_recipe: Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers or 1 chamomile tea bag.
Place the chamomile in a tea infuser or directly into a mug.
Boil 8 ounces of water and pour it over the chamomile.
Steep the chamomile for 5-10 minutes, then remove the infuser or tea bag.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Apigenin: This flavonoid has antispasmodic properties, helping to reduce muscle spasms in the stomach that can cause queasiness.
Bisabolol: This terpene has anti-inflammatory effects, helping to soothe an irritated stomach lining and alleviate feelings of nausea.
Chamazulene: This chemical compound has anti-inflammatory and antiemetic properties, helping to reduce inflammation and prevent vomiting.
Matricin: This sesquiterpene lactone is converted to chamazulene during steam distillation, and has anti-inflammatory effects that can help with queasy stomach.
Hydroxy-chamazulene: This chemical compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping to protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea provides relief for a queasy stomach due to its carminative and anti-inflammatory properties. This herbal tea, derived from the fennel plant, contains volatile oils such as anethole, which relax the gastrointestinal muscles, alleviating discomfort. Fennel tea's hydrophobic properties enable it to dissolve and expel gas, reducing bloating and pain. Moreover, it helps balance stomach acidity, making it an effective remedy for indigestion and acid reflux. Lastly, fennel tea's antispasmodic agents soothe the intestinal lining, thereby minimizing stomach spasms and cramps.
tea_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are excellent for making medicinal tea for queasy stomachs due to their carminative properties that help reduce gas and bloating.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel are beneficial for soothing an upset stomach as they contain volatile oils that promote proper digestion.
Rhyzomes: Fennel rhyzomes make an effective ingredient in medicinal tea for queasy stomachs because they have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help alleviate digestive discomfort.
Flowers: The flowers of the fennel plant contain calming agents that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, making them a valuable addition to medicinal tea for queasy stomachs.
tea_recipe: Chop: Slice 1 tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or 1 teaspoon of crushed fennel seeds.
Steep: Place the fennel seeds in a tea infuser or tea ball and steep in boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain: Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to remove the fennel seeds.
Sweeten (optional): Add honey or sugar to taste for a sweet flavor.
Serve: Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for a queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Anethole: Anethole, a volatile oil in fennel, has carminative properties that help to reduce bloating, gas, and cramps, which can alleviate a queasy stomach.
Fenchone: Fenchone, another volatile oil, acts as an antispasmodic, helping to relax the gastrointestinal muscles and reducing stomach discomfort.
Limonene: Limonene, a component of fennel's essential oil, has been shown to have gastroprotective effects, helping to soothe and protect the stomach lining.
Estragole: Estragole, a volatile oil, has been found to have antiemetic properties, which may help to reduce nausea and vomiting.
Dianethole: Dianethole, a constituent of fennel, has anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which can help to alleviate pain and inflammation in the digestive tract, reducing queasiness.
Methyl chavicol: Methyl chavicol, also known as estragole, helps to increase gastric motility, aiding in digestion and reducing feelings of discomfort and queasiness.

tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe an upset stomach.
Rhizomes: Licorice rhizomes are rich in flavonoids that can help relieve symptoms of queasiness and support digestion.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain various antioxidants and help alleviate digestive discomforts, promoting a calmer stomach.
Flowers: Licorice flowers have been used in traditional medicine to treat nausea and promote healthy digestion due to their carminative properties.
Seeds: Licorice seeds are a popular remedy for an uneasy stomach, as they can help alleviate symptoms such as bloating and cramps.
teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea brings immediate relief to a queasy stomach due to its active ingredient, glycyrrhizin, which boasts potent anti-inflammatory properties. This compound works by reducing inflammation in the stomach lining, thereby alleviating discomfort. Furthermore, glycyrrhizin stimulates the production of mucin, a crucial substance that safeguards the stomach against acid and irritants. The result is a soothed stomach and improved overall digestive health. Moreover, licorice tea boasts carminative effects, facilitating the expulsion of intestinal gas and reducing the likelihood of bloating, a common cause of queasiness. Additionally, this tea's antispasmodic properties help relax the gastrointestinal tract, minimizing muscle spasms and promoting regular bowel movements.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root pieces.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active ingredient in licorice root, known for its anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties. It helps soothe the stomach lining and reduces inflammation, providing relief for queasy stomachs.
Flavonoids: These natural antioxidants help reduce inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract, aiding in alleviating queasiness and promoting overall digestive health.
Triterpenoids: These compounds, including glycyrrhetic acid, have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, helping to prevent and heal stomach ulcers and reduce symptoms of queasy stomachs.
Coumarins: This class of compounds contributes to licorice tea's soothing and calming properties, which can help settle a queasy stomach and reduce feelings of nausea.

Volatile Oils: These essential oils, including anethole and licochalcone A, have antibacterial and antispasmodic properties, aiding in relieving digestive discomfort and preventing nausea, gas, and bloating.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea relieves queasy stomachs due to its demulcent properties, which involve the extraction of mucilage from the tree's bark. This mucilage transforms into a slick, gel-like substance upon contact with water, creating a protective layer in the digestive tract. This lining shields the stomach from acidic irritations, reducing discomfort and inflammation. Furthermore, this soothing coating encourages mucous membrane regeneration, fostering a healthy gastrointestinal system. Ultimately, slippery elm tea's natural components work harmoniously to alleviate queasy stomachs by safeguarding and soothing the digestive tract.
tea_parts: Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for queasy stomach due to its demulcent properties, which mean it has a soothing effect on the membranes of the stomach and intestines.
Roots: The roots of the slippery elm plant are not used to make the tea as they are too harsh and not as effective in soothing the stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of the slippery elm plant are used in teas for their milder, but still effective, mucilage content which can help to coat and soothe the lining of the stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the slippery elm plant are mucilaginous, just like the bark and leaves, making them a good substitute for the inner bark when making tea to soothe a queasy stomach.
tea_recipe: Measure - Pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1 teaspoon of slippery elm bark.
Steep - Let the mixture steep, covered, for 10-15 minutes.
Strain - Strain the tea to remove bark particles.
Stir - Stir in honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Sip - Drink the tea slowly, a few sips at a time, to soothe a queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The gel-like substance forms a protective layer in the stomach, soothing irritation and reducing symptoms of nausea.
Tannins: These astringent compounds help to reduce inflammation, alleviate pain, and control diarrhea, contributing to overall digestive health.
Flavonoids: Antioxidant properties of flavonoids aid in reducing free radical damage, supporting gut health and providing relief from queasy stomach.
Starch: Acting as a demulcent, starch helps soothe and protect the lining of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing discomfort and promoting a healthy digestive process.
Essential fatty acids: Omega-3 and Omega-6 fatty acids aid in reducing inflammation and support the mucosal lining of the stomach, contributing to a healthy digestive system.
Vitamin A: Acting as an anti-inflammatory agent, vitamin A helps in reducing irritation and soothing the stomach lining, offering relief from queasy stomach.
Vitamin C: This vitamin supports overall immune function and encourages the healing of gastrointestinal tissues, promoting digestive health and alleviating nausea.
Calcium: Calcium contributes to normal muscle function, supporting healthy digestive motility and reducing the likelihood of a queasy stomach.
Magnesium: Magnesium aids in reducing stomach spasms and encourages normal muscle function, promoting optimal digestive health and reducing nausea.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the Marshmallow plant's root, contains a mucilage that soothes upset stomachs. This mucilage forms a protective layer on the stomach lining, reducing irritation. Moreover, it boasts anti-inflammatory properties, further alleviating discomfort. Marshmallow tea's demulcent qualities ensure hydration, aiding the body in recovering from stomach ailments. Consequently, queasy stomachs find relief as the tea's constituents work harmoniously to decrease inflammation, soothe irritation, and promote overall stomach health.
tea_parts: Roots: The marshmallow root contains mucilage, which soothes the stomach lining, providing relief for queasy stomachs.
Leaves: Marshmallow leaves, with their anti-inflammatory properties, help alleviate irritation and settle upset stomachs.
Flowers: Rich in flavonoids, marshmallow flowers offer soothing relief for queasy stomachs due to their calming properties.
Seeds: Marshmallow seeds contain high amounts of mucilage, forming a protective barrier in the digestive tract, helping to ease queasy stomachs.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 tablespoon of freshly grated ginger, then bring to a boil and let simmer for 10 minutes.
Add 1 peppermint tea bag and 1 chamomile tea bag to the pot, then turn off the heat and let steep for 5 minutes.
Remove the tea bags and stir in 1 tablespoon of honey until fully dissolved.
Squeeze half a lemon into the pot and stir well.
Pour the tea through a strainer into a mug, then top off with 2-3 marshmallows for added sweetness before serving.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow tea coats the stomach lining, which can help protect it from irritants and soothe a queasy stomach.
Polysaccharides: These complex carbohydrates can help bulk up stools and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach, such as diarrhea and cramping.
Flavonoids: Certain flavonoids, such as rutin and quercetin, found in marshmallow tea can help reduce inflammation, which can contribute to a queasy stomach.

teas.tea_desc: Skullcap tea brings relief to a queasy stomach due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the digestive tract's muscles, reducing discomfort. This herbal tea's calming effect on the nervous system helps alleviate stress-induced stomach issues, promoting overall digestive health. The anti-inflammatory compounds in skullcap tea address any inflammation in the stomach lining, soothing the queasiness. Furthermore, it has been scientifically proven to regulate gut bacteria, ensuring a balanced digestive process and preventing sensations of nausea. Lastly, the mild sedative properties of skullcap tea facilitate better sleep, allowing the body to rest and recover from stomach irritations.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the skullcap plant contain antispasmodic properties that aid in soothing a queasy stomach.
Flowers: The flowers of the skullcap plant have calming compounds that alleviate queasiness.
Stems: The stems of the skullcap plant possess tannins that help reduce inflammation in the stomach, thereby reducing queasiness.
Seeds: The seeds of the skullcap plant have therapeutic properties that help settle an upset stomach.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the skullcap plant have medicinal benefits that help alleviate symptoms of an uneasy stomach.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 teaspoon of crushed skullcap herb with 1 cup of water.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat and let the tea steep for an additional 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the herbs, then allow it to cool slightly.
Sip the skullcap tea slowly to soothe a queasy stomach.
tea_constituents: Flavonoids: These antioxidants have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce irritation in the stomach, providing relief from queasiness.
Tannins: These astringent compounds can help reduce stomach inflammation and minimize mucous membrane secretion, alleviating queasy stomach.
Volatile oils: The soothing effects of these oils can help relax the stomach muscles, providing relief from queasiness and stomach discomfort.
Sesquiterpene lactones: These compounds can aid in digestion and may help reduce symptoms of nausea and queasiness.
Alkaloids: With their antispasmodic properties, alkaloids can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing queasiness and promoting healthy digestion.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea, brewed from the leaves and flowers of Nepeta cataria, historically aids queasy stomachs due to its volatile oil content, particularly nepetalactone. This compound notably relaxes the gastrointestinal tract, diminishing discomfort and promoting normal digestive functioning. Moreover, catnip demonstrates antispasmodic qualities that alleviate involuntary muscle spasms in the gut, frequently associated with queasiness. Furthermore, its calming effects on the nervous system reduce stress-induced stomach issues. Lastly, catnip's mild carminative action dispels intestinal gas, thereby soothing an upset stomach.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the catnip plant contain oils and chemicals that soothe internal discomforts, making them an effective ingredient for medicinal tea to alleviate queasy stomachs.
Leaves: Catnip leaves are rich in calming properties, making them a popular choice for treating stomach queasiness when brewed into a medicinal tea.
Flowers: Adorned with delicate blooms, catnip flowers possess natural oils that help settle uneasy stomachs when steeped into a medicinal tea.
Buds: Unopened flowering tips, or buds, hold potent medicinal qualities, making them a suitable addition to a calming tea to ease feelings of queasiness.
tea_recipe: Infuse one teaspoon of dried catnip leaves in boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the mixture to remove leaves, retaining the liquid.
Squeeze half a lemon into the catnip tea for added soothing properties.
Add honey or sugar to taste, if desired.
Consume the tea while warm, up to three times a day for stomach comfort.
tea_constituents: Nepetalactone: Known for its anti-spasmodic properties, it helps reduce stomach cramps and spasms, providing relief from queasy stomach.
Valepotriates: Act as natural nervine and sedative agents, helping to reduce stress and anxiety that can exacerbate queasy stomach symptoms.
Nepetalic acid: Exhibits anti-inflammatory effects, which can help alleviate inflammation in the digestive tract, thereby soothing a queasy stomach.
Tannins: Possess astringent qualities, helping to protect the mucous membranes of the stomach and reduce irritation, thereby alleviating queasiness.
Rosmarinic acid: Functions as a natural antioxidant, helping to reduce oxidative stress in the digestive system and ease queasy stomach symptoms.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea's ability to soothe a queasy stomach stems from its inherent properties. This herbal tea, derived from the dandelion plant, contains natural bitter compounds that stimulate digestive juices, promoting healthy digestion and alleviating queasiness. Furthermore, its richness in antioxidants aids in maintaining a healthy gut, reducing the likelihood of stomach discomfort. Dandelion tea's diuretic properties also help eliminate toxins from the body, thereby minimizing potential stomach irritants. Additionally, its soothing warmth comforts the stomach, reducing uneasiness and promoting a sense of calm.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in medicinal compounds, the roots soothe the stomach lining, alleviating queasiness.
Leaves: High in antioxidants, the leaves help settle the stomach, reducing feelings of queasiness.
Flowers: Packed with soothing properties, dandelion flowers ease queasy stomachs, promoting digestive comfort.
tea_recipe: Gather 2-3 fresh dandelion heads, ensuring they are from a pesticide-free area, and rinse them under cold water.
Separate the yellow petals from the green base and stems, as the green parts can cause a bitter taste.
Place the petals in a tea infuser or teapot and add 1 cup of boiling water, allowing them to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the infuser or strain the petals from the tea.
Sip the dandelion tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your queasy stomach. Enjoy within 24 hours for maximum freshness.
tea_constituents: Taraxacin: This chemical helps stimulate digestion and can alleviate symptoms of a queasy stomach.
Taraxacerin: This compound found in dandelion tea can help regulate digestion and reduce symptoms of an upset stomach.
Choline: Choline can help soothe a queasy stomach by supporting the health of the digestive system and reducing inflammation.
Inulin: This type of fiber in dandelion tea can help regulate digestion, improve gut health, and reduce symptoms of an upset stomach.
Potassium: Potassium can help regulate fluid balance in the body, which can reduce symptoms of a queasy stomach and help with digestion.
Bitter Glycosides: These compounds found in dandelion tea can help stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of a queasy stomach.
Tannins: Tannins have an astringent effect, which can help soothe a queasy stomach and reduce inflammation.
Sesquiterpene Lactones: These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of a queasy stomach.
